Acme Community Arts Initiative

This short packet is a flat born-digital document intended for intake smoke testing. It has no table of contents, no authored chapter structure, and no printed page numbers. The maintained non-TOC PDF lane should still produce a coherent `doc-web` bundle instead of stopping at `no-recipe-needed`.

The initiative invites neighborhood choirs, youth ensembles, and volunteer organizers to propose small public performances for the autumn season. Each proposal should identify the event name, the lead contact, the expected audience, and any equipment requests. Applicants may also describe accessibility needs, venue constraints, and rehearsal preferences in plain prose.

The review committee scores submissions on readiness, community relevance, and production complexity. Strong submissions are complete, easy to trace back to named organizers, and realistic about staffing. The committee does not require a formal cover sheet as long as the essential facts are stated somewhere in the packet.

Requested information:

Please include the project title, the lead organizer, an email address, a phone number, and the preferred event month. If the proposal includes minors, list the supervising adult and the expected supervision ratio. If the proposal includes amplified sound, note whether the venue must provide microphones, speakers, or a mixing board.

Budget notes:

Small grants may cover modest printing, transport, and venue support costs. They do not cover performer salaries, touring, or permanent equipment purchases. Applicants may list donated goods or volunteer labor separately so the review committee can distinguish direct costs from in-kind support.

Review notes:

Projects that involve shared neighborhood history, intergenerational participation, or free public access are preferred when proposals are otherwise comparable. The committee may request clarifications by email before a final decision, but it will not rewrite incomplete submissions on behalf of applicants.

Submission checklist:

Confirm that the packet identifies the event concept, the organizer, the intended venue type, and at least one reachable contact method. Confirm that the packet states whether tables, chairs, or audio support are needed. Confirm that the packet names any partners or sponsoring groups that should appear in the final announcement if the proposal is accepted.

Operational details:

If the proposal anticipates outdoor use, identify a weather backup and explain who is responsible for a same-day cancellation decision. If the proposal expects audience overflow, describe whether a second seating, an alternate room, or a waitlist process is realistic. If the proposal depends on borrowed instruments, list the lender and any handling requirements.

Communications notes:

Accepted projects will receive a short approval email followed by a production planning call. Organizers should be prepared to confirm title spelling, participant names, and the preferred public blurb for posters or website listings. If a project name is provisional, say so explicitly in the packet to avoid reuse of a draft title.

Accessibility notes:

The review committee encourages plain-language event descriptions, wheelchair-accessible venue planning, and early disclosure of interpretation or sensory-support needs. If a project expects a standing audience, say whether seating can be reserved for elders, caregivers, or people with mobility constraints.

Archival notes:

If the proposal includes original music, oral history, or community documentation, indicate whether the organizers expect a recording, a photo gallery, or a simple text summary afterward. The packet does not need release forms at intake time, but it should mention any anticipated consent or privacy constraints so the final production plan is realistic.

Final reminder:

This fixture is intentionally modest and generic. It exists to prove that the maintained non-TOC born-digital lane can carry flat text-rich PDFs all the way to an inspected bundle even when there is no authored TOC, no explicit chapter map, and no printed page numbering for downstream structure to lean on.
